exemplar.title:
How can mathematical modelling that employs 
differential equations determine population growth 
patterns for a Wolf and an Elk?

detailedExemplarData.title:
How can mathematical modelling that employs 
differential equations determine population growth 
patterns for a Wolf and an Elk?

subject.title: Mathematics Analysis and Approaches (AA)
subject.description:
**IB Mathematics Analysis and Approaches** is a **Group 5** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **number and algebra, functions, geometry and trigonometry, statistics and probability, and calculus**, with a strong focus on **theoretical understanding, formal mathematical reasoning, and analytical problem-solving**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2021**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Non-calculator written exam | 90 minutes | 120 minutes | 40% |
| **Paper 2** | Calculator allowed written exam | 90 minutes | 120 minutes | 40% |
| **Paper 3** | Calculator allowed problem-solving | N/A | 60 minutes | 20% (HL only) |
| **Mathematical Exploration** | Internal Assessment | 20 hours | 20 hours | 20% |

strengths: Introduction communicates the ecological context and modelling purpose clearly and effectively.
Research question is precisely stated and remains tightly connected to each analysis section.
Methodology is complete, with appropriate selection of LotkaâVolterra ODEs, data sources, and Bayesian inference workflow.
weaknesses: Minor lapses in expression (e.g., pluralization âWolfsâ) slightly reduce polish.
Occasional digressions into background material marginally weaken conciseness.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The studentâs focus and method align strongly with the top band: the topic is communicated accurately, the question is both narrowly focused and explicitly linked to subsequent analysis, and the methodological pathway is fully articulated. Minor linguistic slips do not undermine overall coherence, justifying the maximum mark of 6.

strengths: Demonstrates deep understanding of predatorâprey ODEs, Jacobian analysis, and Bayesian estimation.
Terminology such as âJacobian matrixâ, âequilibrium pointâ, and âNUTSâ is used accurately and consistently.
Effective integration of ecological data and mathematical notation throughout.
weaknesses: A few conceptual slips (e.g., eigenvalue sign interpretation) and notation inconsistencies (q vs. Î±).
Occasional minor formatting errors in equations (extra parentheses, missing determinants).
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Although there are some conceptual and notational imperfections, the application of subject knowledge is consistently relevant and underpins the modelling and inference. Terminology is used with precision. These strengths place the work in the highest band for Knowledge and Understanding, meriting 6 marks.

strengths: Incorporates relevant population data and secondary literature to frame the inverse problem.
Analysis is clear, with phase-plane and trace-plot visuals supporting well-supported conclusions.
Reasoned discussion of model assumptions, limitations, and future extensions shows evaluative thinking.
weaknesses: Some numerical and biological validation issues (e.g., unrealistic population predictions) are not fully interrogated.
Key diagnostics (e.g., R-hat, effective sample sizes) and hyperparameter justifications are missing.
Occasional arithmetic slips in eigenvalue calculations weaken the rigor.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The work exhibits very strong critical thinking: research is consistently relevant, analysis is effective, and the argument is coherent and evaluative. However, gaps in diagnostic reporting, numerical validation, and a few analytical slips prevent a perfect score in this criterion, leading to 11 out of 12.

strengths: Logical IMRAD structure with numbered headings, table of contents, and reflections enhances readability.
Equations, tables, and figures are integrated; word count and code appendices are provided.
Consistent formatting supports clear navigation of the report.
weaknesses: Minor layout inconsistencies in figure captions and table alignment.
Bullet formatting and equation numbering vary slightly in style.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Presentation meets all requirements for clarity and structure. The minor formatting lapses do not detract from readability, justifying the full 4 marks in this criterion.

strandSelections.reasoning: The research question is explicitly stated in quotation marks and is narrow enough to be addressed by a single predator-prey model with parameter estimation.  Subsequent sections (Jacobian analysis, Bayesian inversion, evaluation) relate directly back to this question, confirming a strong connection between question and discussion.  Although the wording is a little verbose, it is still clear and focused rather than broad or ambiguous.

The student derives equilibrium conditions, constructs the Jacobian, solves the characteristic polynomial, and implements MCMC estimation with prior justification.  Graphical outputs (phase-plane, trace plots) support the narrative, and conclusions link back to model behaviour.  Depth is strong; nevertheless, arithmetic slips and limited biological validation prevent a move to the top band.

Specialist terminologyâJacobian matrix, equilibrium point, NUTS, log-likelihoodâis used frequently and mostly accurately; definitions are provided for key symbols.  Occasional inaccuracies (âWolfsâ, mis-stated characteristic matrix) prevent the usage from being fully consistent, keeping the performance in the middle band.

Equations are typeset, tables and figures are integrated, and word count plus personal code are provided.  Minor layout lapses (inconsistent figure captions, occasional table misalignment) are noticeable but do not seriously hinder readability, so the work satisfies most layout conventions.

The essay demonstrates good understanding of predator-prey ODEs, Jacobians and Bayesian estimation, applying them with mostly correct notation.  However, there are conceptual slips (incorrect sign interpretation of eigenvalues, biologically implausible sample calculation giving 21 000 elk lost per unit time) that weaken consistent relevance.  Overall knowledge is sound but not excellent.

The introduction clearly states the ecological context, outlines why the wolfâelk pair was chosen, and provides an accessible explanation of differential-equation modelling.  Headings, figures and an explicit purpose (âto investigate how mathematical modelling â¦ can determine population growth patternsâ) keep the focus visible throughout, so the reader is never in doubt about what is being explored.  Minor lapses in expression (âWolfsâ) and some digressions into background material slightly reduce the overall sharpness, but the topic remains accurately and effectively communicated.

The essay follows a logical IMRAD-style structure with clear numbered headings, a table of contents, and separate reflection sections, all of which aid understanding.  All required elements are present and supportive of the reader.

Population data for 2010-2020 are incorporated, and a substantial body of secondary mathematical literature is consulted.  The data directly feed the Bayesian inverse problem, showing strong relevance to the question.  Some uncertainties around data provenance and quality stop the work short of âexcellentâ, but overall research is good and mostly relevant.

A reasoned discussion of assumptions, limitations, coding challenges and future extensions shows evaluative thinking.  Yet evaluation of the numerical output is brief (e.g., unrealistic predicted populations not interrogated), so critical depth is uneven.  The argument is therefore partially critical rather than fully rigorous.

The student specifies the LotkaâVolterra equations, explains assumptions, gathers a decade of population data, and designs an MCMC/Stan workflow for parameter inference.  Sources are cited for both ecological data and computational techniques, showing informed selection of methods.  While some numerical results are unrealistic, the methodological pathway (ODE â likelihood â Bayesian inference) is complete and appropriate for the question.

annotations.comment: Enrich the caption by specifying model parameters or context to clarify why the depicted phase-plane shows neutral stability.
The text refers to parameter q in P = r/q, but the model uses Î±; replace q with Î± to maintain notation consistency.
Include the explicit Jacobian matrix expression and its eigenvalue calculation here to support the classification discussion.
The title is prominently presented at the start, clearly stating the investigation focus.
Including the word count (3761) meets IB formatting requirements.
The detailed table of contents enhances navigability and supports clear structure through separation of sections.
The term âWolfsâ should be corrected to âwolvesâ to ensure accuracy in terminology.
The research question is clearly stated in quotation marks, maintaining strong focus and connection to subsequent analysis.
Consider supplementing general online tutorials with peer-reviewed ecological or mathematical journal articles to enhance methodological rigor.
The citation âKrainesâ lacks full reference details; ensure in-text citations correspond to full entries in the Works Cited.
Maintain consistent list formatting by aligning bullet symbols and indentation for parameter definitions.
Place equation numbers uniformly in parentheses at the right margin to improve consistency in equation formatting.
Consider adding a discussion of seasonal or temporal variability to address temporal assumptions for population dynamics.
Provide a brief rationale or source for choosing the hypothetical parameter value r = 0.6 to support the realism of the example calculation.
Specify the time unit (e.g., years, months) associated with dN/dt and dP/dt for clarity in interpreting rates.
Add numerical eigenvalues alongside Figure 4 to explicitly demonstrate the stability classification for this equilibrium.
Could you clarify the units or dimensions of the Jacobian matrix entries in relation to time and population variables?
Equation (7) repeats the matrix instead of its determinant; include the expression det(...) = Î»Â² - 0.18 directly for clarity.
Solving Î»Â² - 0.18 = 0 yields Î» = Â±â0.18 (real), yet neutral stability requires purely imaginary eigenvalues; re-examine the characteristic equation or sign convention.
Include units (e.g., individuals, yearâ»Â¹) for population counts and parameter rates in Table 1.1 to enhance clarity.
Equation (13) contains an extra closing parenthesis; verify parentheses around the integral for mathematical accuracy.
Justify the chosen hyperparameters of the Gamma and LogNormal priors with ecological literature or sensitivity analysis.
Report convergence diagnostics (e.g., R-hat, effective sample size) for the NUTS chains to verify posterior sampling quality.
The inferred predation rate Î± â 2.66Ã10â»âµ per interaction seems biologically implausible given typical predator-prey dynamics; discuss potential reasons.
Add a marker and annotation for the equilibrium point within the phase-plane figure to guide reader interpretation.
Directly reference Figure 7 in this section to connect the discussion of trace plots to the visual output.
